annotations.label: Trigonometric functions

anchor.snippet: are real functions which relate an angle of a right-angled triangle to ratios of two side lengths

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `Real.sin`, `Real.cos`, `Real.tan` (and their complex analogues) via the complex exponential rather than as right-triangle ratios.

annotations.label: Six trigonometric functions

anchor.snippet: most commonly used in modern mathematics are the sine

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Real.sin`, `Real.cos`, `Real.tan` and `Complex.cot` (see `Mathlib.Analysis.SpecialFunctions.Trigonometric.Cotangent`) but does not appear to define standalone `sec`/`csc` functions.

annotations.label: Right-angled triangle definitions

anchor.snippet: Thus these six ratios define six functions of

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Real.sin`/`Real.cos` are defined through the complex exponential rather than as opposite/adjacent/hypotenuse ratios in a right triangle.

annotations.label: Cofunction identity from complementary angles

anchor.snippet: the sum of the two acute angles is a right angle

annotations.note: The cofunction identities `sin(π/2 − x) = cos x` and `cos(π/2 − x) = sin x` are proved as `Real.sin_pi_div_two_sub` and `Real.cos_pi_div_two_sub`.

annotations.label: Radian measure

anchor.snippet: the angle is given as the length of the arc of the unit circle subtended by it

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `Real.Angle` as the quotient `ℝ/2πℤ` rather than constructing radians from arc length on the unit circle.

annotations.label: Unit-circle definitions of sine and cosine

anchor.snippet: defined, respectively, as the x - and y -coordinate values of point A

annotations.note: Mathlib has the unit-circle map `Circle.exp` (with `exp_mul_I = cos + sin·I`) but does not take the unit-circle coordinates as the definition of `sin`/`cos`.

annotations.label: Pythagorean identity from unit circle

anchor.snippet: this definition of cosine and sine also satisfies the Pythagorean identity

annotations.note: Proved as `Real.sin_sq_add_cos_sq` (and `Complex.sin_sq_add_cos_sq`).

anchors.snippet: this definition of cosine and sine also satisfies the Pythagorean identity

annotations.label: Tangent, cotangent, secant, cosecant in terms of sine and cosine

anchor.snippet: these definitions can readily be shown to coincide with the definitions of tangent, cotangent, secant and cosecant in terms of sine and cosine

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `Real.tan x = sin x / cos x` and proves `Real.tan_eq_sin_div_cos`; cotangent is `cos/sin` (`Complex.cot_eq_cos_div_sin`).

annotations.label: Periodicity of trigonometric functions

anchor.snippet: trigonometric functions are periodic functions with period

annotations.note: `Real.sin_periodic` and `Real.cos_periodic` establish `Function.Periodic … (2 * π)`.

annotations.label: Fundamental period of tangent and cotangent

anchor.snippet: the tangent function and the cotangent function have a fundamental period of

annotations.note: `Real.tan_periodic : Function.Periodic tan π` exists but I found no statement that π is the smallest positive (fundamental) period.

annotations.label: Algebraic values at standard angles

anchor.snippet: Writing the numerators as square roots of consecutive non-negative integers

annotations.note: Specific values like `cos_pi_div_four`, `sin_pi_div_six`, `cos_pi_div_eight`, … are all proved in `Trigonometric/Basic.lean`.

annotations.label: Constructibility of sine and cosine for multiples of 3 degrees

anchor.snippet: may be expressed in terms of square roots. These values of the sine and the cosine may thus be constructed by ruler and compass

annotations.note: I found no Mathlib statement linking sin/cos of 3°-multiples to straightedge-and-compass constructibility.

annotations.label: Galois obstruction for integer-degree angles

anchor.snippet: Galois theory allows a proof that, if the angle is not a multiple of 3°, non-real cube roots are unavoidable

annotations.note: I found no Galois-theoretic result of this form in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Algebraicity for rational-degree angles

anchor.snippet: the sine and the cosine are algebraic numbers , which may be expressed in terms of n -th roots

annotations.note: I found no statement that sin/cos of rational-degree angles are algebraic numbers.

annotations.label: Transcendence corollary of Baker's theorem

anchor.snippet: either the angle or both the sine and the cosine are transcendental numbers

annotations.note: Only the analytical part of Lindemann's theorem is present (`NumberTheory/Transcendental/Lindemann/AnalyticalPart`); no Baker-style transcendence corollary for sin/cos was found.

annotations.label: Rational tangent implies rational double-angle sine and cosine

anchor.snippet: if the tangent of an angle is rational then both the sine and cosine of the double angle will be rational

annotations.note: Tangent half-angle formulas exist but I found no statement specifically asserting rationality of `sin 2θ`/`cos 2θ` from rationality of `tan θ`.

annotations.label: Sine and cosine as initial value problem

anchor.snippet: can be defined as the unique solution to the initial value problem

annotations.note: Mathlib does not characterise sin/cos as solutions of an IVP; they are defined via the exponential.

anchors.snippet: can be defined as the unique solution to the initial value problem

annotations.label: Sine and cosine solve the harmonic ODE

anchor.snippet: both sine and cosine are solutions of the same ordinary differential equation

annotations.note: First and second derivatives of sin/cos are formalized (`hasDerivAt_sin`, `deriv_cos = -sin`) so y'' = -y is immediate but I found no explicit ODE statement.

anchors.snippet: both sine and cosine are solutions of the same ordinary differential equation

annotations.label: Periodicity from ODE defines pi

anchor.snippet: One can then prove, as a theorem, that solutions

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `Real.pi` analytically and does not derive its existence from periodicity of ODE solutions.

annotations.label: Tangent ODE

anchor.snippet: so the tangent function satisfies the ordinary differential equation

annotations.note: `hasDerivAt_tan : HasDerivAt tan (1 / cos x ^ 2) x` is proven, giving `tan' = 1 + tan²` up to algebra, but no ODE statement is recorded.

anchors.snippet: so the tangent function satisfies the ordinary differential equation

annotations.label: Power series for sine and cosine

anchor.snippet: The basic trigonometric functions can be defined by the following power series expansions

annotations.note: `Real.sin_eq_tsum`, `Real.cos_eq_tsum`, and `Complex.{sin,cos}_eq_tsum` express the standard power series.

anchors.snippet: The basic trigonometric functions can be defined by the following power series expansions

annotations.label: Entire extension via infinite radius of convergence

anchor.snippet: The radius of convergence of these series is infinite

annotations.note: `Complex.differentiable_sin`/`differentiable_cos` show entirety, but I found no explicit `formalPowerSeries.radius = ⊤` statement.

anchors.snippet: The radius of convergence of these series is infinite

annotations.label: Meromorphic extension of other trig functions

anchor.snippet: the other trigonometric functions may be extended to meromorphic functions

annotations.note: `Complex.meromorphic_tan` (and `meromorphic_tanh`) establish meromorphicity on ℂ.

annotations.label: Combinatorial interpretation of Taylor coefficients

anchor.snippet: they enumerate alternating permutations of finite sets

annotations.note: I found no connection between tangent/secant Taylor coefficients and alternating permutations in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Continued fraction expansions

anchor.snippet: The following continued fractions are valid in the whole complex plane

annotations.note: No continued-fraction expansion of sin/cos/tan appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Partial fraction expansion of cotangent

anchor.snippet: There is a series representation as partial fraction expansion where just translated reciprocal functions are summed up

annotations.note: The Mittag-Leffler expansion `π·cot(πz) = 1/z + Σ (1/(z−n) + 1/(z+n))` is the goal of `Trigonometric/Cotangent.lean`.

anchors.snippet: There is a series representation as partial fraction expansion where just translated reciprocal functions are summed up

annotations.label: Euler's infinite product for sine

anchor.snippet: The following infinite product for the sine is due to Leonhard Euler

annotations.note: `Complex.tendsto_euler_sin_prod` and `Real.tendsto_euler_sin_prod` prove `sin(πz) = πz·∏(1 − z²/n²)`.

anchors.snippet: The following infinite product for the sine is due to Leonhard Euler

annotations.label: Euler's formula

anchor.section: Euler's formula and the exponential function
anchor.snippet: Euler's formula relates sine and cosine to the exponential function

annotations.note: `Complex.exp_mul_I : exp (x * I) = cos x + sin x * I`.

anchors.section: Euler's formula and the exponential function
anchors.snippet: Euler's formula relates sine and cosine to the exponential function

annotations.label: Sine and cosine in terms of complex exponential

anchor.section: Euler's formula and the exponential function
anchor.snippet: Solving this linear system in sine and cosine, one can express them in terms of the exponential function

annotations.note: The `Complex.sin` and `Complex.cos` definitions themselves are `(exp(−zI) − exp(zI))·I/2` and `(exp(zI) + exp(−zI))/2`.

anchors.section: Euler's formula and the exponential function
anchors.snippet: Solving this linear system in sine and cosine, one can express them in terms of the exponential function

annotations.label: Topological group definition of sine and cosine

anchor.section: Euler's formula and the exponential function
anchor.snippet: Euler's formula can also be used to define the basic trigonometric function directly

annotations.note: No definition of sin/cos via abstract topological-group homomorphism characterizations is present.

anchors.section: Euler's formula and the exponential function
anchors.snippet: Euler's formula can also be used to define the basic trigonometric function directly

annotations.label: Inverse tangent via integration

anchor.snippet: Another way to define the trigonometric functions in analysis is using integration

annotations.note: `integral_one_div_one_add_sq` gives `∫ 1/(1+x²) = arctan b − arctan a`, but arctan is defined via inverse of tan rather than as this integral.

anchors.snippet: Another way to define the trigonometric functions in analysis is using integration

annotations.label: Sine and cosine by inversion

anchor.snippet: the trigonometric functions are defined by inverting the relation

annotations.note: Mathlib does not build sin/cos by inverting an integral relation.

anchors.snippet: the trigonometric functions are defined by inverting the relation

annotations.label: Addition formulae from integral definition

anchor.snippet: one may first establish the addition formulae

annotations.note: Addition formulae exist in Mathlib but not as derivations from an integral characterization.

anchors.snippet: one may first establish the addition formulae

annotations.label: Functional equation characterization of sine and cosine

anchor.snippet: the sine and the cosine form the unique pair of continuous functions that satisfy the difference formula

annotations.note: No uniqueness theorem characterizing sin/cos by their functional equations was found.

anchors.snippet: the sine and the cosine form the unique pair of continuous functions that satisfy the difference formula

annotations.label: Complex sine and cosine via real and hyperbolic functions

anchor.snippet: can be expressed in terms of real sines, cosines, and hyperbolic functions as follows

annotations.note: `Complex.sin_eq` and `Complex.cos_eq` express `sin z`/`cos z` in terms of real `sin`, `cos`, `sinh`, `cosh` of the real and imaginary parts.

anchors.snippet: can be expressed in terms of real sines, cosines, and hyperbolic functions as follows

anchors.value: {\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}\sin z&=\sin x\cosh y+i\cos x\sinh y\\[5pt]\cos z&=\cos x\cosh y-i\sin x\sinh y\end{aligned}}}

annotations.label: Periodicity and smallest positive period

anchor.snippet: The sine and cosine functions are periodic , with period

annotations.note: Periodicity with period `2π` is formalized via `sin_periodic`/`cos_periodic` but minimality of the period is not stated explicitly.

anchors.snippet: The sine and cosine functions are periodic , with period

annotations.label: Zeros of sine and cosine

anchor.snippet: All of the zeros are simple zeros

annotations.note: `Real.sin_eq_zero_iff` characterises sin zeros as integer multiples of π, and `cos_eq_zero_iff` similarly for cos; simplicity not explicitly stated.

anchors.snippet: All of the zeros are simple zeros

annotations.label: Tangent zeros and poles

anchor.snippet: The tangent function

annotations.note: Zeros of tan at integer multiples of π are formalized (`tan_int_mul_pi`) and meromorphicity captures poles, but a unified zeros-and-poles statement isn't given.

anchors.snippet: The tangent function

annotations.label: Cotangent zeros and poles

anchor.snippet: The cotangent function

annotations.note: `Complex.cot` is defined and meromorphic, but I found no explicit characterisation of its zeros and poles.

anchors.snippet: The cotangent function

annotations.label: Parity of trigonometric functions

anchor.snippet: The cosine and the secant are even functions ; the other trigonometric functions are odd functions

annotations.note: `cos_neg`, `sin_neg`, and `tan_neg` formalise even/odd parities of cos/sin/tan.

anchors.snippet: The cosine and the secant are even functions ; the other trigonometric functions are odd functions

anchors.value: {\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}\sin(-x)&=-\sin x\\\cos(-x)&=\cos x\\\tan(-x)&=-\tan x\\\cot(-x)&=-\cot x\\\csc(-x)&=-\csc x\\\sec(-x)&=\sec x.\end{aligned}}}

annotations.label: Periods of trigonometric functions

anchor.snippet: All trigonometric functions are periodic functions of period 2

annotations.note: `sin_periodic`, `cos_periodic` (period `2π`) and `tan_periodic` (period `π`) are all formalised.

anchors.snippet: All trigonometric functions are periodic functions of period 2

anchors.value: {\displaystyle {\begin{array}{lrl}\sin(x+&2k\pi )&=\sin x\\\cos(x+&2k\pi )&=\cos x\\\tan(x+&k\pi )&=\tan x\\\cot(x+&k\pi )&=\cot x\\\csc(x+&2k\pi )&=\csc x\\\sec(x+&2k\pi )&=\sec x.\end{array}}}

annotations.label: Pythagorean identity

anchor.snippet: The Pythagorean identity, is the expression of the Pythagorean theorem in terms of trigonometric functions

annotations.note: `Real.sin_sq_add_cos_sq : sin x ^ 2 + cos x ^ 2 = 1`.

anchors.snippet: The Pythagorean identity, is the expression of the Pythagorean theorem in terms of trigonometric functions

annotations.label: Sum and difference formulas

anchor.snippet: The sum and difference formulas allow expanding the sine, the cosine, and the tangent of a sum or a difference of two angles

annotations.note: `sin_add`, `cos_add`, `sin_sub`, `cos_sub`, and tangent analogues are all in `Analysis.Complex.Trigonometric`.

annotations.label: Double-angle formulae

anchor.snippet: When the two angles are equal, the sum formulas reduce to simpler equations known as the double-angle formulae

annotations.note: `cos_two_mul`, `cos_two_mul'`, `sin_two_mul`, and `cos_two_mul_eq_one_sub` formalise the double-angle identities.

annotations.label: Tangent half-angle substitution

anchor.snippet: this is the tangent half-angle substitution , which reduces the computation of integrals and antiderivatives of trigonometric functions to that of rational fractions

annotations.note: The half-angle formulas for sin/cos in terms of `tan(x/2)` are formalised both for ℂ and ℝ.

anchors.snippet: this is the tangent half-angle substitution , which reduces the computation of integrals and antiderivatives of trigonometric functions to that of rational fractions

annotations.label: Derivatives of trigonometric functions

anchor.snippet: The derivatives of trigonometric functions result from those of sine and cosine by applying the quotient rule

annotations.note: `hasDerivAt_sin`, `hasDerivAt_cos`, `hasDerivAt_tan`, `deriv_sin`, `deriv_cos` give derivatives for sin, cos, tan.

annotations.label: Inverse trigonometric functions

anchor.snippet: on each interval on which a trigonometric function is monotonic , one can define an inverse function

annotations.note: `Real.arcsin`, `Real.arccos`, and `Real.arctan` (in `Trigonometric/Arctan.lean`) are defined as restricted inverses.

annotations.label: Law of sines

anchor.snippet: The law of sines states that for an arbitrary triangle with sides

annotations.note: `sin_angle_div_norm_eq_sin_angle_div_norm` (vector form) and `sin_angle_div_dist_eq_sin_angle_div_dist` (point form) prove the law of sines.

anchors.snippet: The law of sines states that for an arbitrary triangle with sides

annotations.label: Law of cosines

anchor.snippet: The law of cosines (also known as the cosine formula or cosine rule) is a generalization of the Pythagorean theorem

annotations.note: Both vector-angle and angle-at-point forms of the law of cosines are proved in `Geometry/Euclidean/Triangle.lean`.

anchors.snippet: The law of cosines (also known as the cosine formula or cosine rule) is a generalization of the Pythagorean theorem

anchor.snippet: The law of tangents says that

annotations.note: I found no law-of-tangents statement in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: The law of tangents says that

annotations.label: Law of cotangents

anchor.snippet: The law of cotangents says that

annotations.note: I found no law-of-cotangents statement in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: The law of cotangents says that

annotations.label: Heron's formula via incircle radius

anchor.snippet: Heron's formula implies that

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Affine.Simplex.inradius` but no Heron's formula relating it to side lengths and area.

annotations.label: Fourier series of periodic functions

anchor.snippet: a periodic function f ( x ) can be expressed as a sum of sine waves or cosine waves in a Fourier series

annotations.note: Fourier coefficients/series for L²-functions on `AddCircle` are formalised (`Mathlib.Analysis.Fourier.AddCircle`), but no general convergence-of-Fourier-series theorem for arbitrary periodic functions is identified.

anchors.snippet: a periodic function f ( x ) can be expressed as a sum of sine waves or cosine waves in a Fourier series

annotations.label: Square wave Fourier series

anchor.snippet: the square wave can be written as the Fourier series

annotations.note: No square-wave Fourier-series example was found in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: the square wave can be written as the Fourier series

annotations.label: Leibniz: sine is not algebraic

anchor.snippet: Gottfried Leibniz proved that sin x is not an algebraic function of x

annotations.note: I found no statement that `sin` is not an algebraic function of `x` in Mathlib.
